chapter.title: JSON and XML to ECL Tools
chapter.para: The HPCC Systems Client Tools include utilities to scan JSON and XML data files and generate ECL record definitions. These command-line tools simplify the process of integrating external data sources by automatically generating ECL RECORD structures from sample data files.

Both tools are available in the client tools package and on bare metal installations in the /opt/HPCCSystems/bin directory. The general purpose of both is to scan data files and derive an ECL RECORD structure that can read those files.

sect1.title: json2ecl
sect1.para: json2ecl [OPTIONS] [FILES...]

The json2ecl utility examines JSON data and deduces the ECL RECORD definitions necessary to parse it. The tool supports both standard JSON documents and NDJSON (Newline Delimited JSON) format. This tool analyzes the structure of JSON input and generates corresponding ECL RECORD definitions with XPATH annotations suitable for use with ECL's JSON() dataset format.
JSON data can be supplied as one or more files or via standard input. The resulting ECL definitions are returned via standard output.

For efficiency, when scanning arrays or NDJSON streams, the tool stops processing after 500 consecutive elements that don't modify the schema. This prevents unnecessary processing of large uniform datasets while ensuring schema accuracy. Use --full-scan to scan all elements if needed.

When multiple files are provided, they are parsed as if they have the same record structure. This is useful when not all fields are defined in a single file, allowing the tool to discover the complete schema across all files.
The tool automatically detects and handles NDJSON format. When multiple top-level JSON objects are detected, the NOROOT flag is automatically added to the generated ECL code.

row.entry: One or more JSON files to process. Multiple files are parsed as if they have the same record structure, useful when not all fields are defined in a single file. If omitted, data is read from standard input.
Specify the ECL string type to use for string fields. Valid options are: UTF8, STRING, VARSTRING, UNICODE. Default: UTF8

Scan all array elements or NDJSON elements without limit. By default, for efficiency, the tool stops processing after 500 consecutive elements that don't modify the schema. Use this option to scan all elements if needed.

listitem.para: Quoted numbers (e.g., "1234") are treated as numbers, not strings.
JSON null values cannot be distinguished from empty strings.
Field names containing spaces may not be handled correctly due to underlying JSON parser behavior (e.g., 'this is a test' may become 'this_sis_sa_stest'). Use underscore or camelCase naming instead.
The tool automatically detects field types from the JSON data (strings, integers, floating-point numbers, booleans, objects, arrays).
Nested objects are converted to child RECORD structures with automatically generated names.
Arrays are handled appropriately based on their content type (primitive arrays or arrays of objects).
All generated fields include XPATH annotations that correspond to the JSON path to that field.
ECL reserved words used as field names are automatically prefixed with 'f_' to avoid conflicts.
The default string type is UTF8. Use the -s option to change to STRING, VARSTRING, or UNICODE as needed.
When processing multiple files, ensure they represent the same logical data structure with different field coverage.

para.emphasis: Scan JSON data from a file

sect1.para: Reads JSON data from data.json and writes the ECL record definition to standard output.
Reads JSON from standard input and outputs the ECL record definition.

para.emphasis: Process multiple files with STRING type

sect1.para: Processes multiple JSON files, combining their schemas, and generates ECL record definitions using STRING type instead of the default UTF8.
Generates the ECL record definition and saves it to layout.ecl.

para.emphasis: Scan all elements in large file

sect1.para: Processes all elements in the JSON file without the default 500 element limit.
Given a JSON file foo.json with the following content:
Running the command:
Generates the following ECL output:
Note that the generated code includes:

listitem.para: XPATH annotations for each field indicating the JSON path to the data
Appropriate ECL types based on the JSON values (UTF8 for strings, UNSIGNED for positive integers, REAL for floating-point numbers)
A commented-out sample DATASET declaration showing how to use the generated layout
Field names prefixed with 'f_' if they conflict with ECL reserved words (e.g., 'end' becomes 'f_end')

sect1.para: ECL record structures generated by json2ecl can be used directly with ECL's DATASET declaration using the JSON() format:
For more complex structures with nested records:

listitem.para: Review Generated Code: Always review the generated ECL code. The tools provide a starting point, but you may need to refine field types or lengths based on your specific requirements.

Use Representative Samples: Ensure your sample data files include representative examples of all field variations. The tools analyze the data to determine schema, so incomplete samples may result in incomplete schemas.
Process Multiple Files: If your data has optional fields that don't appear in every record, process multiple representative files together to capture the complete schema.
Test with Real Data: Always test the generated record definitions with your actual data to verify correct parsing and type handling.
Handle Large Files: For very large files, the default 500-element scan limit is usually sufficient. Only use --full-scan if you suspect the schema changes throughout the file.
String Type Selection: Choose the appropriate string type (-s option) based on your data. UTF8 is the default and recommended for most use cases, especially with international data.
Handle Reserved Words: Be aware that ECL reserved words in field names are automatically prefixed. Check the generated code for these prefixes (e.g., 'f_' prefix).
Save Generated Layouts: Save the generated ECL layouts to files and include them in your version control system for tracking changes over time.
XML Namespaces: For XML files with namespaces, you may need to manually clean up or handle the xmlns:* attribute fields in the generated code.

sect1.title: xml2ecl
sect1.para: The xml2ecl utility examines XML data and deduces the ECL RECORD definitions necessary to parse it. This tool analyzes XML structure and generates corresponding ECL RECORD definitions with XPATH annotations suitable for use with ECL's XML() dataset format or PARSE function.
XML data can be supplied as one or more files or via standard input. The resulting ECL definitions are returned via standard output.

row.entry: One or more XML files to process. Multiple files are parsed as if they have the same record structure, useful when not all fields are defined in a single file. If omitted, data is read from standard input.
Specify the ECL string type to use for string fields. Valid options are: UTF8, STRING, VARSTRING, UNICODE. Default: UTF8

Scan all array elements without limit. By default, for efficiency, the tool stops processing after 500 consecutive elements that don't modify the schema. Use this option to scan all elements if needed.

sect1.para: xml2ecl examines XML data and deduces the ECL RECORD definitions necessary to parse it. The tool analyzes XML element structure, attributes, and content to generate appropriate ECL RECORD definitions.

Multiple Files: When multiple files are provided, they are parsed as if they have the same record structure. This is useful when not all fields are defined in a single file, allowing the tool to discover the complete schema across all files.
Attribute Handling: XML attributes are included in the schema with their original names. When an element has both attributes and text content, the text is stored in a field named '_inner_value'.

Performance: For efficiency, when scanning repeated elements, the tool stops processing after 500 consecutive elements that don't modify the schema. This prevents unnecessary processing of large uniform datasets while ensuring schema accuracy. Use --full-scan to scan all elements if needed.

listitem.para: Namespace declarations (xmlns:* attributes) are preserved as fields in the generated ECL schema.
Mixed content (text and elements interleaved) may not be fully supported.

sect1.para: Examples:

para.emphasis: Scan XML data from a file

sect1.para: Reads XML data from data.xml and writes the ECL record definition to standard output.
Reads XML from standard input and outputs the ECL record definition.

para.emphasis: Process multiple files with STRING type

sect1.para: Processes multiple XML files, combining their schemas, and generates ECL record definitions using STRING type instead of the default UTF8.
Generates the ECL record definition and saves it to layout.ecl.

para.emphasis: Scan all elements in large file

sect1.para: Processes all elements in the XML file without the default 500 element limit.
Given an XML file customer.xml with the following content:
Running the command:
Generates ECL output with:

listitem.para: XPATH annotations for each field indicating the XML path to the data
Appropriate ECL types based on the XML content (UTF8 for text, UNSIGNED for positive integers)
Nested RECORD structures for nested XML elements (e.g., Address)
XML attributes included as fields with their original names (e.g., id)
A commented-out sample DATASET declaration showing how to use the generated layout

sect1.para: When an XML element contains both attributes and text content, the text content is stored in a field named '_inner_value'.

listitem.para: The tool automatically detects field types from the XML data (text content, numeric values, nested elements).
Nested elements are converted to child RECORD structures with automatically generated names.
Repeating elements (arrays) are handled appropriately with XPATH annotations.
XML attributes are included in the schema with their original names.
When an element has both attributes and text content, the text is stored in a field named '_inner_value'.
All generated fields include XPATH annotations that correspond to the XML path to that field.
The default string type is UTF8. Use the -s option to change to STRING, VARSTRING, or UNICODE as needed.
Namespace declarations (xmlns:* attributes) are preserved as fields. You may need to manually remove or handle these in your ECL code.
When processing multiple files, ensure they represent the same logical data structure with different field coverage.

sect1.para: Alternatively, using the PARSE function for more control:

listitem.para: Review Generated Code: Always review the generated ECL code. The tools provide a starting point, but you may need to refine field types or lengths based on your specific requirements.

Use Representative Samples: Ensure your sample data files include representative examples of all field variations. The tools analyze the data to determine schema, so incomplete samples may result in incomplete schemas.
Process Multiple Files: If your data has optional fields that don't appear in every record, process multiple representative files together to capture the complete schema.
Test with Real Data: Always test the generated record definitions with your actual data to verify correct parsing and type handling.
Handle Large Files: For very large files, the default 500-element scan limit is usually sufficient. Only use --full-scan if you suspect the schema changes throughout the file.
String Type Selection: Choose the appropriate string type (-s option) based on your data. UTF8 is the default and recommended for most use cases, especially with international data.
Handle Reserved Words: Be aware that ECL reserved words in field names are automatically prefixed. Check the generated code for these prefixes (e.g., 'f_' prefix).
Save Generated Layouts: Save the generated ECL layouts to files and include them in your version control system for tracking changes over time.
XML Namespaces: For XML files with namespaces, you may need to manually clean up or handle the xmlns:* attribute fields in the generated code.
